schools.summary: Paul's request for their intercession: 'pray for us, that the word of the Lord may have free course (trecho — may run, speed onward swiftly, like a runner)' — that the gospel may advance unhindered, spreading rapidly; 'and be glorified (honoured, received with honour)' — that the word may not only spread but be honoured and prized wherever it goes; 'even as it is with you' — the Thessalonians' own experience of the word's free course and glorification the pattern of his prayer (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: pray for us, that the word of the Lord may have free course, and be glorified
even as it is with you
Paul's request for prayer for the gospel's advance
the word running and glorified

schools.summary: Paul's request for personal deliverance: 'that we may be delivered (rescued) from unreasonable (atopon — out of place, perverse, absurd, beyond the bounds of reason or fairness) and wicked (evil, malicious) men' — from the perverse and hostile opponents who threatened his work and safety (likely the Jews at Corinth, Acts 18), whose opposition was both irrational and malicious; 'for all men have not faith (the faith)' — not all receive or hold the gospel, the opposition arising because faith is not universal and the faithless resist the truth (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: that we may be delivered from unreasonable and wicked men
for all men have not faith
the prayer for deliverance from wicked men
opposition because faith is not universal

schools.summary: The turn from faithless men to the faithful Lord: 'but the Lord is faithful (pistos — trustworthy, true to his promises)' — over against the men who 'have not faith' (v.2), the Lord is full of faithfulness (a play on the word); 'who shall stablish (sterixei — make firm, strengthen, confirm) you' — he will steady and strengthen them, the very thing Paul prayed for (2:17); 'and keep (guard, preserve) you from evil (apo tou ponerou — from the evil one, Satan, or from evil generally)' — the Lord their protector against the evil one's assaults (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).

themes: But the Lord is faithful, who shall stablish you, and keep you from evil
the faithful Lord will stablish and keep them
faithful Lord over against faithless men
kept from the evil one

schools.summary: Paul's confidence rests not in them but in the Lord: 'we have confidence in the Lord touching you (concerning you)' — his trust in their obedience grounded in the Lord who works in them, confidence 'in the Lord,' not in human steadfastness alone; 'that ye both do (now obey) and will do (will continue to obey) the things which we command you' — confident of both their present and future obedience to his apostolic instructions, on which the charges that follow (vv.6-15) are given; he prefaces his commands with this expression of confidence, gently disposing them to obey (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: we have confidence in the Lord touching you
that ye both do and will do the things which we command you
Paul's confidence in their obedience
trust grounded in the Lord

schools.summary: Paul's prayer: 'the Lord direct (kateuthynai — make straight, guide, lead) your hearts' — that the Lord would guide and incline their inmost affections in the right path; 'into the love of God' (whether God's love to them or, most take it, their love to God, into which the Lord directs their hearts); 'and into the patient waiting for Christ (hypomonen tou Christou)' — either the patient expectation of Christ's coming, or (more literally) the patience/steadfast endurance of Christ, the endurance Christ himself showed and gives (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: And the Lord direct your hearts into the love of God, and into the patient waiting for Christ
the prayer that the Lord direct their hearts
into the love of God
the patience of Christ

schools.summary: The solemn weight of the charge: 'we command (parangellomen — charge, enjoin with authority) you... in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ' — given not as advice but as an authoritative apostolic command, backed by Christ's name; 'that ye withdraw yourselves (stellesthai — draw back, keep aloof, avoid) from every brother that walketh disorderly (ataktos — out of rank, idly, irregularly)' — the 'disorderly' being chiefly the idle busybodies (v.11) who, expecting Christ's imminent coming, had quit work; 'and not after the tradition (the apostolic teaching and example) which he received of us,' the apostolic tradition being the measure of orderly walking (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: that ye withdraw yourselves from every brother that walketh disorderly
and not after the tradition which he received of us
the command to withdraw from the disorderly
keeping aloof from the idle

schools.summary: Paul points to his own conduct as the model: 'for yourselves know how ye ought to follow (mimeisthai — imitate) us: for we behaved not ourselves disorderly (ataktos — idly, out of rank) among you' — they knew, from his example, the orderly, industrious life they should imitate; Paul holds up his own example as the standard of orderly walking, the rule he commands (v.6) he himself first kept, his preaching and conduct agreeing so that his blameless example gave weight to his command (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: For yourselves know how ye ought to follow us
for we behaved not ourselves disorderly among you
Paul's own orderly example
practice matching preaching

schools.summary: Paul's example in detail: 'neither did we eat any man's bread for nought (without paying, as a free charge)' — he took no free maintenance; 'but wrought (worked) with labour and travail (kopos kai mochthos — toil and hardship) night and day' — labouring at his trade (tentmaking), constant and wearying toil to support himself, so that his preaching might cost the church nothing (the same self-denial as 1 Thessalonians 2:9); 'that we might not be chargeable (a burden, a financial charge) to any of you' — that none be burdened, and that he might leave an example of industry (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: neither did we eat any man's bread for nought
but wrought with labour and travail night and day, that we might not be chargeable to any of you
Paul's self-supporting toil
labouring not to burden them

schools.summary: Paul's careful qualification: 'not because we have not power (exousia — the right, the authority)' — he did have the right, as an apostle, to be supported by the churches (1 Corinthians 9:4-14), and did not forego it because he lacked the claim; 'but to make ourselves an ensample (typon — a pattern, model) unto you to follow (imitate) us' — he supported himself precisely to set an example of industry, that they might copy his diligence and not be idle, giving up a lawful right for their good so that his example might teach more powerfully than words (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: not because we have not power, but to make ourselves an ensample unto you to follow us
Paul foregoing his right to set an example
waiving a lawful right for their good
an example of industry

schools.summary: The rule was no new invention: 'for even when we were with you, this we commanded you' — Paul had laid down this rule during his ministry at Thessalonica, the present letter only enforcing what he had taught; 'that if any would not (ou thelei — is unwilling to, refuses to) work, neither should he eat' — the careful wording targeting willful idleness, not those who cannot find work or are unable; the refusal of the will is the point, the able-bodied idler having no claim on the charity of the church but must work to eat (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley).

themes: if any would not work, neither should he eat
even when we were with you, this we commanded you
the rule that he who will not work should not eat
willful idleness, not inability

schools.summary: The reason for the charge: 'for we hear (it is reported) that there are some which walk among you disorderly (ataktos — out of rank, idly)' — a specific problem of idleness had arisen; 'working not at all, but are busybodies (meddlers)' — a pointed play on words in the Greek (ergazomenous / periergazomenous): they did no proper work of their own, yet were busy meddling in others' affairs, idleness breeding officious interference; the idleness probably sprang from their mistaken excitement about Christ's imminent coming, which led them to abandon work (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: there are some which walk among you disorderly, working not at all, but are busybodies
the report of idle busybodies
idleness breeding meddling
the play on words: not busy but busybodies

schools.summary: Paul's balanced address to the idlers: 'we command (charge with authority) and exhort (parakaloumen — entreat, encourage) by our Lord Jesus Christ' — both commanding and entreating, joining apostolic authority with brotherly persuasion in Christ's name; 'that with quietness (hesychias — quietly, peaceably, settling down) they work' — the cure for the restless meddling (v.11), settling to quiet, steady labour, quietness set against busybody bustle; 'and eat their own bread' — bread earned by their own labour, the honest fruit of their own work, not charity or another's toil (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley).

themes: that with quietness they work, and eat their own bread
the charge to the idle to work quietly
quietness over against meddling
eating their own bread

schools.summary: Paul turns from the idlers to the faithful body: 'but ye, brethren, be not weary (ekkakesete — lose heart, grow weary, faint) in well doing (doing good)' — the well-behaved not to grow tired of doing right, whether of their honest labour or their charity; 'well doing' may include both persevering in good conduct and continuing in deeds of kindness, especially not letting the abuse of charity by the idle discourage genuine generosity to the truly needy; the misconduct of some must not chill the well-doing of the rest (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: But ye, brethren, be not weary in well doing
the exhortation to the faithful not to grow weary
persevering in good despite the idlers' abuse
the misconduct of some not chilling the rest

schools.summary: The contingency: 'and if any man obey not (refuses to obey) our word by this epistle' — if any, after this letter, still persists in disobedience to the apostolic command; 'note (semeiousthe — mark, take note of, set a mark upon) that man, and have no company with him (do not associate with him, keep aloof)' — the church to single him out and withdraw ordinary fellowship, that he feel the weight of his fault; 'that he may be ashamed (be put to shame, brought to repentance)' — the goal not destruction but reformation, the shame meant to bring him to his senses and back to right conduct (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley).

themes: if any man obey not our word by this epistle, note that man, and have no company with him
that he may be ashamed
the discipline of marking and avoiding the disobedient
discipline aimed at repentance

schools.summary: The gracious limit: 'yet count him not as an enemy' — the withdrawal of fellowship (v.14) is not enmity; the disobedient brother is not to be treated as a foe, hated or cast off as hopeless; 'but admonish (noutheteite — warn, counsel, set right) him as a brother' — even in discipline he remains a brother, the correction to be done in love with the aim of reclaiming him, not punishing or destroying; church discipline must hold together firmness and love, firm enough to mark the fault yet loving enough to treat the offender still as a brother to be won back (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: Yet count him not as an enemy, but admonish him as a brother
the limit of discipline: admonish, not treat as an enemy
discipline tempered by love
the offender still a brother to be won

schools.summary: The fitting title after the discord he addressed: 'now the Lord of peace himself (the Lord who is the author and source of peace) give you peace always (at all times)' — Paul prays that Christ, the very source of peace, would grant them continual peace; 'by all means (in every way, in every circumstance)' — peace of every kind and in every situation: peace with God, peace among themselves (over against their divisions), and peace within, total and unbroken; 'the Lord be with you all' — his presence the sum of all blessing and the source of all peace (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: Now the Lord of peace himself give you peace always by all means
The Lord be with you all
the prayer to the Lord of peace
peace of every kind and his presence

schools.summary: Paul, who dictated the letter, now adds the closing greeting in his own handwriting: 'the salutation of Paul with mine own hand' — his personal signature, authenticating the letter as genuinely his; 'which is the token (semeion — the sign, mark) in every epistle' — this autograph greeting his standard mark of genuineness, for given the danger of forged letters in his name (2:2), Paul's own hand was the sign by which his true letters could be known; 'so I write' — perhaps pointing to the very style or recognizable form of his handwriting, that they might distinguish his genuine letters from forgeries (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: The salutation of Paul with mine own hand, which is the token in every epistle
so I write
Paul's own-hand salutation
the mark of authenticity against forgery

schools.summary: Paul closes, as he ever does, with grace: 'the grace (free favour, the whole sum of gospel blessing) of our Lord Jesus Christ' — the benediction that seals every letter, he having begun with grace (1:2) and ending with it; 'be with you all' — the comprehensive parting blessing embracing the whole church, every member without exception, including even the disorderly he had just disciplined, grace for all; 'Amen' the seal, the heart's confirmation committing the church to the grace of Christ (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).
Cyril: the closing 'Amen' commits the church to the grace of Christ, the one source of all blessing, with which alone the believer can stand against the deceptions and trials of the last days.

themes: The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all. Amen
the closing benediction of grace
grace for the whole church, even the disciplined
the seal of the epistle
